content.problemStatement:
A permutation of length NNN is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.

Given NNN and KKK, find a permutation PPP of length NNN satisfying the following constraints:


Pi‚Č†iP_i \ne iPi‚ÄčÓÄ†=i for all 1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \le i \le N1‚Č§i‚Č§N
Pi‚Äämod‚ÄäK=i‚Äämod‚ÄäKP_i \bmod K = i \bmod KPi‚ÄčmodK=imodK for all 1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \le i \le N1‚Č§i‚Č§N
In case no such permutation exists, output ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains 222 integers - NNN and KKK.


Output Format
For each test case, output the following:



If a valid permutation exists, output NNN space-separated integers - P1,P2,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1‚Äč,P2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äč, representing the permutation you found.
Otherwise, output ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1.

If there are multiple valid permutations, you may print any of them.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: All the constraints are satisfied with P=[3,4,1,2]P = [3, 4, 1, 2]P=[3,4,1,2]. For example, we can check P1‚Č†1P_1 \ne 1P1‚ÄčÓÄ†=1 but P1‚Äämod‚Ää2=1‚Äämod‚Ää2=1P_1 \bmod 2 = 1 \bmod 2 = 1P1‚Äčmod2=1mod2=1.
Test case 222: Since K=1K = 1K=1, Pi‚Äämod‚ÄäK=i‚Äämod‚ÄäKP_i \bmod K = i \bmod KPi‚ÄčmodK=imodK holds by default. We only need Pi‚Č†iP_i \ne iPi‚ÄčÓÄ†=i, so any permutation satisfying that works. Another possible answer is P=[3,1,2]P = [3, 1, 2]P=[3,1,2].
Test case 333: No valid permutation exists.
